definition: A proposal to enter into a contract on specific terms.
example: The seller made an offer to sell the car for $10,000.

definition: An unqualified agreement to the terms of an offer.
example: The buyer's acceptance of the offer formed a binding contract.

definition: Something of value exchanged between parties to make a contract enforceable.
example: The consideration for the agreement was $500 paid upon signing.

definition: Failure to perform any term of a contract without a legitimate excuse.
example: The contractor's failure to deliver on time constituted a breach of contract.

definition: A monetary award to compensate for loss caused by breach of contract.
example: The court awarded damages of $50,000 for the breach of the supply agreement.

definition: The ending of a contract by one or both parties.
example: The client exercised the right of termination after repeated delays.

definition: A promise that certain facts or conditions are true or will happen.
example: The seller gave a warranty that the goods were free from defects.

definition: An obligation to compensate another party for losses or damages.
example: The indemnification clause required the vendor to cover all third-party claims.

definition: Substitution of a new contract or party for an old one.
example: By novation, the original debtor was replaced by a new obligor.

definition: Transfer of rights or obligations under a contract to a third party.
example: The contractor assigned its payment rights to a financing company.

definition: The substitution of one party for another in a legal right or claim.
example: The insurer exercised subrogation rights against the negligent driver.

definition: A court order requiring a party to perform its contractual obligations.
example: The court ordered specific performance because the property was unique.

definition: A pre-agreed sum payable upon breach, set out in the contract.
example: The contract provided liquidated damages of $1,000 per day of delay.

definition: An unforeseeable event that prevents a party from fulfilling its obligations.
example: The pandemic was invoked as force majeure to excuse non-performance.

definition: A breach significant enough to excuse the other party from performance.
example: Delivering counterfeit goods was held to be a material breach.

definition: A declaration before performance is due that a party will not perform.
example: His statement that he would not deliver constituted an anticipatory breach.

definition: Cancellation of a contract so that parties are restored to their original positions.
example: The court granted rescission due to fraudulent misrepresentation.

definition: Restoration of a benefit conferred on a party to prevent unjust enrichment.
example: She sought restitution of the advance payment after the contract was voided.

definition: A claim for reasonable payment for services rendered in the absence of a contract.
example: The contractor claimed quantum meruit for work done before the contract was signed.

definition: A gain obtained at another's expense without legal justification.
example: The court found unjust enrichment and ordered repayment of the sum.

definition: A doctrine preventing a party from going back on a promise relied upon by another.
example: Promissory estoppel prevented the landlord from withdrawing the rent reduction.

definition: A rule limiting extrinsic evidence to vary a written contract.
example: Under the parol evidence rule, oral negotiations could not alter the written agreement.

definition: A law requiring certain contracts to be in writing to be enforceable.
example: The contract for the sale of land was unenforceable under the Statute of Frauds.

definition: The legal ability of a party to enter into a binding contract.
example: A minor lacks capacity to enter into most contracts.

definition: Unlawful pressure that causes a party to enter a contract against their will.
example: The agreement was voidable because it was signed under duress.

definition: Improper pressure exploiting a relationship to influence a party's decision.
example: The elderly client signed under undue influence of her financial adviser.

definition: A false statement of fact that induces another party to enter a contract.
example: The buyer rescinded the contract due to the seller's misrepresentation about defects.

definition: A doctrine excusing performance when it becomes objectively impossible.
example: Destruction of the subject matter created impossibility of performance.

definition: A doctrine excusing performance when the contract's purpose is destroyed.
example: Cancellation of the coronation frustrated the purpose of the room hire contract.

definition: An event that must occur before a contractual obligation becomes due.
example: Obtaining planning permission was a condition precedent to the purchase.

definition: An event whose occurrence ends an existing contractual obligation.
example: Loss of the license was a condition subsequent that terminated the agreement.

definition: A contractual provision requiring one party to cover the other's losses.
example: The indemnity clause protected the client from supplier negligence claims.

definition: A clause capping the amount recoverable from one party.
example: The limitation of liability clause set the maximum at the contract price.

definition: A clause stating the contract represents the parties' full agreement.
example: The entire agreement clause excluded all prior representations and warranties.

definition: A provision allowing the rest of a contract to stand if one part is invalid.
example: The severability clause ensured the contract remained valid despite the voided term.

definition: Voluntary relinquishment of a known right.
example: By accepting the late payment, the creditor waived the right to charge penalties.

definition: A clause preventing a party from engaging in competing business activities.
example: The non-compete clause prohibited the employee from joining a rival for two years.

definition: A contract obligating parties to keep specified information confidential.
example: The parties signed a non-disclosure agreement before sharing financial data.

definition: The jurisdiction's law that applies to interpret and enforce a contract.
example: The governing law clause specified that English law would apply.

definition: A provision requiring disputes to be resolved by arbitration.
example: Under the arbitration clause, disputes were referred to ICC arbitration.

definition: A document expressing the intent to enter into a future agreement.
example: The parties signed a letter of intent pending completion of due diligence.

definition: A non-binding agreement outlining the terms of a future contract.
example: The memorandum of understanding set out the framework for the joint venture.

definition: Investigation of a business or asset before signing a binding agreement.
example: The buyer conducted full due diligence before signing the acquisition agreement.

definition: Additional purchase price contingent on the target meeting future milestones.
example: The earnout provision linked $2 million of the price to revenue targets.

definition: A third-party arrangement holding assets until conditions are met.
example: The deposit was held in escrow until completion of the transaction.

definition: The right to match any offer received from a third party before the asset is sold.
example: The shareholder's right of first refusal prevented external parties from buying in.

definition: Statements of fact and promises made to induce a party to enter a contract.
example: The seller's representations and warranties covered title and absence of liens.

definition: The final step of a transaction when documents are signed and funds transferred.
example: Closing took place on 1 March after all conditions precedent were satisfied.

definition: A portion of the purchase price retained to cover potential warranty claims.
example: A 10% holdback was retained for 18 months to secure indemnity obligations.

definition: A provision preventing a seller from soliciting other offers during negotiations.
example: The no-shop clause prevented the target from approaching other buyers for 60 days.

definition: Identical copies of a contract signed by different parties.
example: The agreement was signed in counterparts, each being an original.

definition: An obligation to act honestly and fairly in performing a contract.
example: The parties were required to negotiate the renewal terms in good faith.

definition: The highest standard of effort obligating a party to do all it reasonably can.
example: The agent was required to use best efforts to secure a buyer.

definition: A standard requiring a party to take steps that are reasonable in the circumstances.
example: The licensee undertook to use reasonable efforts to commercialise the product.

definition: A clause making deadlines strict, so late performance is a material breach.
example: Because time was of the essence, the two-day delay justified termination.

definition: A contract that has no legal effect from the beginning.
example: A contract for an illegal purpose is void and unenforceable.

definition: A contract valid until one party elects to rescind it.
example: The contract was voidable at the option of the minor.

definition: A standard-form contract offered on a take-it-or-leave-it basis.
example: The consumer challenged the adhesion contract's arbitration clause.

definition: A doctrine voiding contracts with oppressively unfair terms.
example: The court refused to enforce the clause on grounds of unconscionability.

definition: A person who is not a party to a contract but is intended to benefit from it.
example: The employee was a third-party beneficiary of the insurance policy.

definition: The principle that only parties to a contract can sue or be sued under it.
example: The subcontractor could not sue the employer due to lack of privity of contract.

definition: A contract where both parties exchange mutual promises.
example: A sale of goods is a classic bilateral contract: goods for money.

definition: A contract where one party makes a promise in exchange for an act.
example: The reward notice was a unilateral contract accepted by finding the lost dog.

definition: A surety bond ensuring a contractor completes a project.
example: The employer called on the performance bond when the builder abandoned the site.

definition: A bank's commitment to pay a seller upon presentation of specified documents.
example: Payment was secured by an irrevocable letter of credit issued by the buyer's bank.

definition: A party that guarantees another's performance of an obligation.
example: The parent company acted as surety for its subsidiary's lease obligations.

definition: A clause imposing a deterrent payment that may be unenforceable if penal.
example: The court held the clause was a penalty, not liquidated damages, and struck it out.

definition: A debtor's right to reduce a claim by an amount the creditor owes them.
example: The buyer exercised set-off, deducting repair costs from the purchase price.

definition: Combining multiple obligations to produce a single net amount payable.
example: The ISDA master agreement provided for close-out netting on default.

definition: A clause making default on one agreement an event of default on another.
example: The cross-default clause triggered acceleration on all facilities.

definition: A provision making a debt immediately payable upon occurrence of a default event.
example: Following the borrower's insolvency, the acceleration clause was invoked.

definition: A court order compelling or restraining a party from a specific action.
example: The court granted an injunction to stop the defendant from disclosing trade secrets.

definition: A court ruling clarifying the legal rights of parties without ordering any action.
example: The claimant sought a declaratory judgment that the clause was unenforceable.

definition: A contract giving one party the right to enter a transaction at a future date.
example: The option agreement gave the developer the right to buy the land within 12 months.

definition: Rights allowing a majority shareholder to force minorities to join a sale.
example: The drag-along rights allowed the 75% holder to compel sale to the acquirer.

definition: Rights allowing minority shareholders to join a majority sale on the same terms.
example: Tag-along rights protected minority investors from being left behind in the exit.

definition: A provision protecting an investor's ownership percentage from future issuances.
example: The anti-dilution clause adjusted the conversion price after the down round.

definition: The process by which rights to assets or shares accrue over time.
example: The founder's shares were subject to a four-year vesting schedule.

definition: A vesting arrangement where rights accrue all at once after a minimum period.
example: No shares vested before the one-year cliff under the employee incentive plan.

definition: A transaction resulting in a new party acquiring control of a company.
example: The change of control clause allowed the counterparty to terminate on acquisition.

definition: A deposit paid to demonstrate seriousness of intent to complete a contract.
example: The buyer paid earnest money of $5,000 which would be forfeited on withdrawal.

definition: A contractual promise to do or refrain from doing something.
example: The borrower breached the financial covenant by exceeding the leverage ratio.

definition: A statement of present fact made to induce entry into a contract.
example: The seller's representation that accounts were accurate proved to be false.

definition: A primary obligation to compensate another for specified losses.
example: The indemnity covered all costs arising from pre-completion tax liabilities.

definition: A licence granted by a licensee to a third party.
example: The software licensee granted a sub-licence to its affiliated company.

definition: A payment made for the use of intellectual property or natural resources.
example: The inventor received a 5% royalty on all sales under the licence agreement.

definition: A specific event or deliverable triggering payment or other obligation.
example: Payment of $100,000 was due upon achievement of the first milestone.

definition: A contract defining the standard of service a provider must deliver.
example: The SLA required 99.9% uptime with penalties for each hour of downtime.

definition: A specified time allowed to remedy a breach before the contract is terminated.
example: The defaulting party had a 30-day cure period to remedy the payment failure.

definition: The right to end a contract without cause on giving notice.
example: The client terminated for convenience, paying only the agreed wind-down costs.

definition: Ending a contract because the other party has committed a specified breach.
example: Termination for cause was triggered by the supplier's insolvency.

definition: A provision keeping certain obligations in force after contract termination.
example: The survival clause kept confidentiality obligations alive for five years post-termination.

definition: The other party to a contract or financial transaction.
example: The bank assessed the counterparty's creditworthiness before execution.

definition: A contract where obligations on both sides remain to be performed.
example: The trustee in bankruptcy chose to adopt the executory contract.

definition: A contract so one-sided or oppressive that a court may refuse to enforce it.
example: The court declined to enforce the unconscionable contract signed by an illiterate borrower.

definition: The legal ability to enter into a binding contract.
example: Contractual capacity requires the party to be of legal age and sound mind.

definition: The two core elements forming a binding agreement.
example: Offer and acceptance were established by the exchange of signed term sheets.

definition: A conflict arising when parties exchange contradictory standard terms.
example: The battle of the forms was resolved by applying the last shot rule.

definition: A term not expressly stated but incorporated into a contract by law or custom.
example: An implied term of merchantability applied to the sale of goods.

definition: A term explicitly agreed by the parties, whether orally or in writing.
example: The delivery date was an express term of the sale contract.

definition: A serious crime typically punishable by imprisonment of more than one year.
example: Armed robbery is classified as a felony carrying up to 20 years in prison.

definition: A less serious crime punishable by fines or imprisonment up to one year.
example: Petty theft was charged as a misdemeanor and resulted in a fine.

definition: A formal charge issued by a grand jury that there is probable cause to try a person.
example: The grand jury returned an indictment on three counts of fraud.

definition: A court proceeding where the defendant is formally charged and enters a plea.
example: At arraignment, the defendant pleaded not guilty to all charges.

definition: A security deposit allowing a defendant to be released from custody pending trial.
example: Bail was set at $50,000 to ensure the defendant's appearance at trial.

definition: The mental element of a crime; the intention or knowledge of wrongdoing.
example: The prosecution must prove mens rea beyond a reasonable doubt.

definition: The physical act or conduct constituting a criminal offence.
example: The actus reus of theft is the taking of another's property.

definition: A verdict or judgment that a defendant is not guilty of the charges.
example: The jury returned an acquittal after finding insufficient evidence.

definition: A formal declaration that someone is guilty of a criminal offence.
example: His prior conviction for theft was presented as aggravating evidence.

definition: An agreement between prosecution and defence to plead guilty in exchange for leniency.
example: The defendant accepted a plea bargain, pleading guilty to a lesser charge.

definition: The party, usually the state, that brings criminal charges against a defendant.
example: The prosecution presented DNA evidence linking the accused to the crime.

definition: A lawyer who represents the accused in criminal proceedings.
example: Defence counsel challenged the admissibility of the confession.

definition: The standard of proof required for a criminal conviction.
example: The jury was instructed to convict only if guilt was proven beyond reasonable doubt.

definition: Reasonable grounds to believe a crime has been or is being committed.
example: The officer had probable cause to arrest after finding stolen goods.

definition: A court order authorising law enforcement to search a specific location.
example: Police obtained a search warrant before entering the premises.

definition: A court order authorising the arrest of a named individual.
example: An arrest warrant was issued after the suspect failed to appear in court.

definition: A writ requiring that a detained person be brought before a court.
example: The prisoner filed a habeas corpus petition challenging the legality of his detention.

definition: Early release from prison subject to conditions and supervision.
example: The prisoner was released on parole after serving half his sentence.

definition: A court-ordered period of supervision in the community instead of imprisonment.
example: The judge sentenced the offender to two years' probation with community service.

definition: The tendency of a convicted criminal to reoffend.
example: High recidivism rates prompted reform of the rehabilitation programme.

definition: A person who assists in the commission of a crime.
example: The accomplice drove the getaway car and was charged accordingly.

definition: A person who assists a criminal before or after the offence.
example: She was convicted as an accessory after the fact for hiding the stolen goods.

definition: An agreement between two or more persons to commit a criminal act.
example: All five defendants were charged with conspiracy to import narcotics.

definition: The killing of one person by another.
example: The investigation determined the death was a criminal homicide.

definition: The unlawful killing of a person without premeditation.
example: He was convicted of involuntary manslaughter after the fatal accident.

definition: The unlawful killing of another person with premeditated intent.
example: The defendant was charged with first-degree murder.

definition: An act causing another person to reasonably apprehend imminent unlawful force.
example: Raising a fist and threatening to strike constituted assault.

definition: Unlawful physical contact with another person without consent.
example: Striking the victim constituted battery under criminal law.

definition: Theft from a person using force or the threat of force.
example: Armed robbery carries a mandatory minimum of five years in prison.

definition: Unlawful entry into a building with intent to commit a crime.
example: The defendant was convicted of burglary after breaking into the warehouse.

definition: The unlawful taking and carrying away of another's property.
example: Grand larceny was charged for the theft of property worth over $1,000.

definition: Misappropriation of funds entrusted to a person's care.
example: The treasurer was convicted of embezzlement after diverting $200,000.

definition: Deception intended to result in financial or personal gain.
example: He was convicted of wire fraud for the investment scheme.

definition: Obtaining money or property by threats or coercion.
example: The official was charged with extortion for demanding bribes.

definition: Offering, giving, receiving or soliciting something of value to influence an action.
example: The contractor was convicted of bribery for paying a public official.

definition: The offence of lying under oath in a judicial proceeding.
example: The witness was charged with perjury after contradicting her earlier testimony.

definition: Actions that interfere with the administration of justice.
example: Destroying evidence amounted to obstruction of justice.

definition: Concealing the origins of illegally obtained money.
example: The bank was prosecuted for facilitating money laundering through shell companies.

definition: A body of citizens that reviews evidence to decide whether to charge a suspect.
example: The grand jury deliberated for two weeks before issuing the indictment.

definition: A group of citizens who determine guilt or innocence at trial.
example: The petit jury of twelve returned a unanimous verdict of not guilty.

definition: The jury's formal decision on whether the defendant is guilty.
example: The verdict of guilty was delivered after four days of deliberation.

definition: The punishment imposed by a court on a convicted defendant.
example: The judge imposed a sentence of five years without eligibility for parole.

definition: Factors that reduce the severity of a sentence though not excusing the offence.
example: The defendant's lack of prior convictions was a mitigating circumstance.

definition: Factors that increase the severity of a criminal sentence.
example: The use of a weapon was treated as an aggravating circumstance.

definition: A justification for using force to protect oneself from imminent harm.
example: The defendant claimed self-defence, arguing the force used was proportionate.

definition: A defence claiming the defendant lacked mental capacity to form criminal intent.
example: The court rejected the insanity defence after psychiatric evaluation.

definition: A defence claiming law enforcement induced the defendant to commit an offence.
example: The defence argued entrapment because an undercover agent initiated the transaction.

definition: Evidence that the defendant was elsewhere when the crime was committed.
example: CCTV footage confirmed the defendant's alibi.

definition: The prohibition on trying a person twice for the same offence.
example: The acquittal barred retrial on the same charges under double jeopardy.

definition: The time limit within which criminal charges must be brought.
example: The statute of limitations barred prosecution for the fifteen-year-old offence.

definition: Wilful disobedience or disrespect toward a court order or proceedings.
example: The witness was held in contempt of court for refusing to answer questions.

definition: Attempting to influence a witness's testimony by intimidation or bribery.
example: The defendant was charged with witness tampering for contacting the victim.

definition: Loss of property as a penalty for criminal conduct.
example: Civil forfeiture proceedings were brought against the drug dealer's assets.

definition: A court order requiring a convicted person to compensate the victim.
example: The court issued a restitution order requiring payment of $15,000 to the victim.

definition: Sentences for multiple offences served simultaneously.
example: The two five-year terms were ordered to run concurrently.

definition: Sentences for multiple offences served one after the other.
example: The judge ordered consecutive sentences totalling fifteen years.

definition: A plea that does not admit guilt but accepts the punishment.
example: The defendant entered a nolo contendere plea to avoid a civil judgment.

definition: The initial court appearance where charges are read and a plea entered.
example: At arraignment, the defendant pleaded not guilty and bail was set.

definition: A gross deviation from the standard of care that a reasonable person would observe.
example: The driver's criminal negligence caused the fatal accident.

definition: Liability imposed without proof of fault or intent.
example: Speeding offences impose strict liability regardless of the driver's intent.

definition: Assisting or encouraging another person to commit a crime.
example: She was convicted of aiding and abetting the robbery by acting as lookout.

definition: Inciting or requesting another person to commit a crime.
example: He was charged with solicitation for hiring someone to commit arson.

definition: An act done with intent to commit a crime that falls short of completion.
example: He was charged with attempted murder after the victim survived.

definition: A defence that admits the act but justifies or excuses the defendant's conduct.
example: Self-defence is an affirmative defence requiring the defendant to produce evidence.

definition: Evidence that tends to clear the defendant of guilt.
example: The prosecution was required to disclose all exculpatory evidence to the defence.

definition: Evidence tending to establish guilt.
example: The incriminating evidence found at the scene included the defendant's fingerprints.

definition: The documented sequence of possession of evidence from collection to court.
example: The defence challenged the chain of custody of the blood sample.

definition: Electronic surveillance of communications, usually requiring a court order.
example: Conversations recorded via wiretap were admitted as evidence at trial.

definition: Theft of property above a statutory value threshold.
example: Grand theft auto carries a penalty of up to three years in prison.

definition: Non-violent crime committed by business or government professionals for financial gain.
example: Insider trading is a classic example of white-collar crime.

definition: A crime motivated by prejudice against a protected characteristic.
example: The attack was prosecuted as a hate crime due to the racial slurs used.

definition: Criminal activity carried out by means of computers or the internet.
example: Hacking into bank accounts is prosecuted as cybercrime.

definition: The illegal manufacture, distribution, or sale of controlled substances.
example: The cartel leader was convicted of drug trafficking and sentenced to life imprisonment.

definition: The criminal act of deliberately setting fire to property.
example: The defendant was convicted of arson after the warehouse fire.

definition: The unlawful taking and detention of a person by force or fraud.
example: The kidnapping charge carried a maximum sentence of life imprisonment.

definition: Any unwanted sexual act committed against another person.
example: The defendant was convicted of sexual assault and placed on the sex offenders register.

definition: Repeated unwanted contact or following causing fear to the victim.
example: The repeated messages and appearances constituted stalking under the law.

definition: Violence or abuse by one person against another in a domestic setting.
example: The prosecutor introduced evidence of a pattern of domestic violence.

definition: The use of violence for political, religious or ideological purposes.
example: The suspects were charged under anti-terrorism legislation.

definition: The crime of betraying one's country, especially by aiding an enemy.
example: Passing classified information to a foreign power constituted treason.

definition: The practice of spying to obtain secret information for a foreign power.
example: The intelligence officer was convicted of espionage.

definition: A pattern of criminal activity run as part of an organised criminal enterprise.
example: The gang leader was charged under racketeering statutes for 20 predicate acts.

definition: Trading securities based on material non-public information.
example: The executive was convicted of insider trading for selling shares before the profit warning.

definition: Illegal non-payment or underpayment of taxes.
example: He was sentenced to three years for tax evasion on income from offshore accounts.

definition: Deliberate destruction or damage to public or private property.
example: The youth was convicted of vandalism for spray-painting the monument.

definition: Unlawful entry onto another person's property without permission.
example: The protester was charged with criminal trespass.

definition: An offence involving disturbance of peace or public safety.
example: The rioters were charged with public order offences.

definition: Release of a convicted person subject to conditions without immediate punishment.
example: The first-time offender received a conditional discharge for twelve months.

definition: Unpaid work performed as part of a non-custodial sentence.
example: The offender was ordered to complete 200 hours of community service.

definition: The legally authorised killing of a person as punishment for a crime.
example: Capital punishment remains legal in several US states for first-degree murder.

definition: A legal entity separate from its owners, capable of owning property and entering contracts.
example: The corporation was incorporated under Delaware law.

definition: A person or entity that owns shares in a corporation.
example: The shareholder voted against the proposed merger at the annual general meeting.

definition: The governing body of a corporation elected by shareholders.
example: The Board of Directors approved the acquisition at its quarterly meeting.

definition: An obligation to act in the best interests of another party.
example: Directors owe a fiduciary duty of loyalty to the corporation and its shareholders.

definition: The document filed to formally create a corporation.
example: The articles of incorporation were filed with the Secretary of State.

definition: Internal rules governing the operation and management of a corporation.
example: The bylaws specified quorum requirements for board meetings.

definition: A distribution of profits to shareholders.
example: The board declared a quarterly dividend of $0.50 per share.

definition: The combination of two companies into a single entity.
example: The merger created the largest bank in the country.

definition: The purchase of one company by another.
example: The acquisition of the startup was completed for $500 million.

definition: An acquisition pursued against the wishes of the target company's management.
example: The board adopted a poison pill to defend against the hostile takeover.

definition: A shareholder rights plan that makes a hostile takeover prohibitively expensive.
example: The company's poison pill allowed existing shareholders to buy shares at a discount.

definition: Lucrative benefits granted to executives upon termination following a takeover.
example: The CEO's golden parachute was worth $20 million.

definition: The first sale of a company's shares to the public.
example: The company raised $2 billion in its initial public offering.

definition: A formal document disclosing information to investors before a securities offering.
example: Investors reviewed the prospectus before subscribing to the IPO.

definition: The ownership interest in a company represented by shares.
example: The venture capitalist acquired 20% equity in exchange for its investment.

definition: Raising capital by borrowing money to be repaid with interest.
example: The company chose debt financing to avoid diluting shareholders.

definition: Raising capital by issuing shares in exchange for investment.
example: The startup used equity financing to fund its expansion plans.

definition: Investment in early-stage high-growth companies in exchange for equity.
example: The startup attracted $5 million in venture capital funding.

definition: Investment in companies not listed on a public stock exchange.
example: The private equity firm acquired the retailer through a leveraged buyout.

definition: Acquisition of a company using a significant amount of borrowed money.
example: The leveraged buyout loaded the target with $800 million of debt.

definition: The creation of an independent company from a division of an existing one.
example: The conglomerate completed a spin-off of its pharmaceutical division.

definition: The sale or disposal of a business unit or asset.
example: The regulator required divestiture of overlapping businesses as a merger condition.

definition: The process of dissolving a company by realising its assets and paying its debts.
example: The court ordered the winding up of the insolvent company.

definition: Converting a company's assets into cash to pay creditors and distribute to shareholders.
example: In liquidation, secured creditors were paid before shareholders received anything.

definition: The inability to pay debts as they fall due.
example: The company filed for administration after months of insolvency.

definition: A legal process allowing individuals or companies to seek relief from debts.
example: The retailer filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy to restructure its debts.

definition: A process where a court-appointed receiver manages a company's assets.
example: The bank placed the borrower into receivership after payment default.

definition: An insolvency procedure protecting a company from creditors while restructuring.
example: The retailer entered administration to allow a sale of the business as a going concern.

definition: The system of rules and practices directing a corporation.
example: Weak corporate governance contributed to the scandal.

definition: The obligation of directors to act with reasonable care and diligence.
example: The director breached his duty of care by approving the investment without analysis.

definition: A presumption that directors make informed, good-faith decisions in the company's interest.
example: The court applied the business judgment rule and declined to second-guess the board.

definition: Holding shareholders personally liable for the corporation's obligations.
example: The court pierced the corporate veil because the company was used to commit fraud.

definition: Protection of shareholders from personal liability beyond their investment.
example: Limited liability meant shareholders lost only the value of their shares.

definition: A shareholder owning less than 50% of a company's shares.
example: Minority shareholders challenged the related-party transaction.

definition: A shareholder owning more than 50% of a company's shares.
example: The majority shareholder forced through the resolution over minority objections.

definition: Authorization for one person to vote on behalf of a shareholder.
example: The institutional investor submitted a proxy to vote against the remuneration report.

definition: A yearly meeting at which shareholders vote on company matters.
example: The annual general meeting approved the reappointment of auditors.

definition: A shareholders' meeting called outside the regular schedule for urgent matters.
example: An extraordinary general meeting was convened to approve the emergency capital raise.

definition: The minimum number of members required to conduct valid business.
example: Without a quorum, the board meeting was adjourned.

definition: The total value of shares a company is authorised to issue.
example: The company increased its share capital by issuing new shares.

definition: The maximum number of shares a company may issue under its articles.
example: The board resolved to increase the number of authorised shares.

definition: Shares that have been allocated and given to shareholders.
example: Of 10 million authorised shares, 7 million had been issued.

definition: A company's own shares that have been repurchased and held by it.
example: The company held 2 million treasury shares following the buyback programme.

definition: A company's repurchase of its own outstanding shares.
example: The board approved a $500 million share buyback programme.

definition: An offer of new shares to existing shareholders at a discount.
example: The bank launched a rights issue to strengthen its capital base.

definition: Shares with priority over ordinary shares for dividends and capital on winding up.
example: Preference shareholders received their dividend before ordinary shareholders.

definition: Standard shares with voting rights and entitlement to residual profits.
example: Ordinary shareholders voted at the AGM on the proposed acquisition.

definition: A debt instrument that converts into equity upon a trigger event.
example: The startup issued a convertible note that would convert on the next funding round.

definition: A contract between shareholders governing their rights and obligations.
example: The shareholder agreement restricted transfers of shares without consent.

definition: A business arrangement where two or more parties co-operate on a project.
example: The companies formed a joint venture to develop the new software platform.
